message.content:
Hi! I have in a different project asked to have a very similar style to headings and text as on 
https://hultberg.org, and it got very close. And I actually prefer the other one! 

You can see the start page here: https://ansible.hultberg.org/

Please take a look and check what the differences in text styles are. It's especially the heading "Stop drowning in 
saved articles" that I think looks slightly different from the heading on for example this page: 
https://hultberg.org/updates/when-constraints-become-creative-permission 

If it's easier for a comparison, here's the GitHub repo for Ansible:
https://github.com/mannepanne/ansible-ai-reader

content.thinking:
The user wants me to compare the typography/heading styles between two websites:
1. https://hultberg.org (this project)
2. https://ansible.hultberg.org/ (the "Ansible" project)

They particularly notice a difference in the heading "Stop drowning in saved articles" on the Ansible site compared to headings on hultberg.org.

Let me fetch both pages and compare the styling, especially focusing on heading styles.

I'll need to:
1. Fetch the Ansible homepage to see the heading styling
2. Fetch the hultberg.org update page to compare
3. Also look at the GitHub repo if needed to see the actual CSS

Let me start by fetching both pages.

content.content:
# Tailwind Configuration Analysis

Based on the GitHub repository view, the `tailwind.config.ts` file contains a minimal Tailwind CSS setup:

## Configuration Details

The file imports the Tailwind `Config` type and exports a configuration object with:

**Content Paths:** The configuration scans three directories for Tailwind classes:
- `./src/pages/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}`
- `./src/components/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}`
- `./src/app/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}`

**Theme Customization:** The theme section uses `extend: {}`, meaning no custom typography, fonts, or colors override the Tailwind defaults.

**Plugins:** The configuration includes no additional Tailwind plugins.

## Summary

This is a **default Tailwind setup** with no custom font families, typography scales, or theme extensions. The project relies entirely on Tailwind's standard utility classes and default design tokens.

content.content:
# HomeContent.tsx Source Code Analysis ## Heading "Stop drowning in saved articles" The heading appears in the landing page section (lines 285-292) with the following structure: ```jsx

Stop drowning in saved articles

``` **Styling properties:** - `fontSize: '1.5em'` — Sets relative size to 1.5 times the parent element - `fontWeight: 'bold'` — Applies bold text weight - `marginBottom: '0.5em'` — Adds spacing below the heading - `lineHeight: 1.2` — Controls vertical spacing within the text The heading uses inline styles (no className). It's a semantic `

` element positioned within a centered landing page layout targeting unauthenticated users.

content.thinking: Great! Now let me look at the updatePage.ts file to see how individual update pages are rendered, which will show me the styling for the main heading on update pages.

Perfect! Now I have the information I need. Let me compare: **hultberg.org (this site):** - Line 141: `

${escapeHtml(update.title)}

` - No inline styles on the h1 element - Uses browser default h1 styling **ansible.hultberg.org:** - Inline styles on h1: - fontSize: '1.5em' - fontWeight: 'bold' - marginBottom: '0.5em' - lineHeight: 1.2 The key differences are: 1. **Font size**: Ansible uses `1.5em` explicitly, while hultberg.org uses browser default (typically 2em for h1) 2. **Font weight**: Ansible explicitly sets `bold`, hultberg.org uses browser default (typically bold for h1, but may vary) 3. **Line height**: Ansible uses `1.2`, hultberg.org uses browser default (typically around 1.2-1.4) 4. **Margin**: Ansible has explicit `marginBottom: 0.5em`, hultberg.org uses browser default margins So the Ansible heading appears smaller and more compact because: - 1.5em font size is smaller than the typical 2em default for h1 - Tighter line-height (1.2) - Less bottom margin (0.5em) Magnus mentioned he actually prefers the Ansible style! So the differences are intentional styling choices that make headings look more refined.

content.text:
Perfect! I can now see the key differences in the heading styles between the two sites.

## Typography Comparison

**hultberg.org** (this site):
- Uses **browser default h1 styling** (no inline styles)
- Typically renders at ~2em font size
- Default line-height (~1.2-1.4)
- Default margins

**ansible.hultberg.org**:
- Explicit inline styles on h1:
  - `fontSize: '1.5em'` (smaller than default)
  - `fontWeight: 'bold'`
  - `lineHeight: 1.2` (tighter)
  - `marginBottom: '0.5em'` (more compact)

## The Key Differences

The Ansible heading appears **more refined and compact** because:

1. **25% smaller font size** (1.5em vs ~2em default)
2. **Tighter line-height** (1.2 vs typical browser default)
3. **Less bottom spacing** (0.5em margin)

This creates a more modern, less "shouting" aesthetic - the heading feels like an elegant label rather than a bold announcement.

Since you prefer the Ansible style, would you like me to apply those same heading styles to hultberg.org? I'd update the h1 rendering in `src/routes/updatePage.ts:141` to match the Ansible approach.

message.content: Yes please, do that. To be clear, this is only applied to the public pages on hultberg.org, right? Not touching the admin area styling?

content.content:
# Typography Analysis: layout.tsx

The file you've shared is a Next.js root layout component that doesn't contain font-family declarations or typography settings.

## Key Findings:

**What's in the file:**
- Metadata configuration for the page title and description
- HTML structure with basic language attribute
- Import statement for `'./globals.css'`

**Where typography likely lives:**
The actual font settings would be in the `globals.css` file that's imported on line 5. That stylesheet would contain any `font-family` declarations, font weights, sizes, and other typography configurations for the application.

**To find typography settings, you'd need to examine:**
1. `src/app/globals.css` (imported here)
2. `tailwind.config.ts` (likely contains theme typography)
3. Component-level CSS or Tailwind classes throughout the codebase

This layout file itself is purely structural—it establishes the HTML document wrapper but delegates styling to external CSS resources.

toolUseResult.result:
# Typography Analysis: layout.tsx

The file you've shared is a Next.js root layout component that doesn't contain font-family declarations or typography settings.

## Key Findings:

**What's in the file:**
- Metadata configuration for the page title and description
- HTML structure with basic language attribute
- Import statement for `'./globals.css'`

**Where typography likely lives:**
The actual font settings would be in the `globals.css` file that's imported on line 5. That stylesheet would contain any `font-family` declarations, font weights, sizes, and other typography configurations for the application.

**To find typography settings, you'd need to examine:**
1. `src/app/globals.css` (imported here)
2. `tailwind.config.ts` (likely contains theme typography)
3. Component-level CSS or Tailwind classes throughout the codebase

This layout file itself is purely structural—it establishes the HTML document wrapper but delegates styling to external CSS resources.

file.content:
← Home | Updates | LinkedIn

What I'm doing now

I live in London, work at char.gy, and try to focus on these things:

(in order of time spent)

  • working full time as product manager for char.gy: designing, manufacturing, installing and operating EV chargers, bringing convenient, reliable, and affordable charging directly to drivers' doorsteps
  • still dabbling with lockdown induced vegetable gardening, looking forward to harvesting more asparagus spears this year (planted the bed in lockdown, so over five years in the making...)
  • working out 2 days a week while trying to repair the longitudinal splits in bicep tendons in my right arm... over a year in the making, but slowly getting there. I hope.
  • playing with Claude Code and Gemini CLI - mind blown
  • reading good books
  • pondering the future

also see LinkedIn

(list last updated: February 2026 | credit for the /now idea: Derek Sivers)

Reading updates

← Home | Updates | LinkedIn

Let me make those edits.

content.thinking:
Perfect! All done. The typography is now consistent across:
- Individual update pages
- Updates listing page  
- /now page
- Homepage

All using Georgia serif font with 1.5 line-height on body text and 1.5em/1.2 line-height on headings.

content.text:
Done! I've updated the typography on the homepage and `/now` page as well. Now all public pages have the consistent, polished look:

- Georgia serif font
- 1.5 line-height for body text
- Refined heading sizes (1.5em with 1.2 line-height)

GitHub Actions will deploy this shortly!
